coredata.dc:title: Construction of carbon quantum dots sensitized porous carbon nitride/titanium dioxide nanosheets for enhancing visible light photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xinghao
Yang, Zhaoguang
Yang, Ying
Li, Haipu
coredata.dc:description: Porous carbon nitride/titanium dioxide nanosheets (pCN/TNs) were synthesized by annealing the mixtures of urea, oxalic acid, and prepared titanium dioxide nanosheets, and it was then modified by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to produce CQD-pCN/TNs. The novel CQD-pCN/TNs composites possessed excellent photocatalytic performance for the degradation of tetracycline (TC) with 86.2% under 120 min visible light irradiation, which was improved by 54%, 32.2%, and 20.7% compared to the systems with CNs, TNs, or pCN/TNs, respectively. The study on the catalytic mechanism revealed that the enhanced photocatalytic activity was attributed to the expanded absorption wavelength range by CQDs and the inhibited electron-hole recombination by heterojunction structure. The redshift effect could be compensated by increasing the absorption of visible light for the constructed 1% CQD-pCN/TNs. However, excessive redshift of absorption wavelength band could reduce the photocatalytic activity. The •O2 -, h+, •OH, and 1O2 could be generated, and the former two played major roles, while the latter two played supporting roles in the degradation of TC. Furthermore, the CQDs could promote the production of more reactive species. TC molecule was broken down into small molecules via ring-opening reactions or/and the cleavage of the main carbon bond. The antibacterial activities of treaded solutions and eco-toxicities were detected and predicted by bacteriostatic test and ECOSAR model. This work offered a new sight for the design of excellent photo-catalysts for organic pollutants degradation.

head.ce:title: Construction of carbon quantum dots sensitized porous carbon nitride/titanium dioxide nanosheets for enhancing visible light photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Porous carbon nitride/titanium dioxide nanosheets (pCN/TNs) were synthesized by annealing the mixtures of urea, oxalic acid, and prepared titanium dioxide nanosheets, and it was then modified by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to produce CQD-pCN/TNs. The novel CQD-pCN/TNs composites possessed excellent photocatalytic performance for the degradation of tetracycline (TC) with 86.2% under 120 min visible light irradiation, which was improved by 54%, 32.2%, and 20.7% compared to the systems with CNs, TNs, or pCN/TNs, respectively. The study on the catalytic mechanism revealed that the enhanced photocatalytic activity was attributed to the expanded absorption wavelength range by CQDs and the inhibited electron-hole recombination by heterojunction structure. The redshift effect could be compensated by increasing the absorption of visible light for the constructed 1% CQD-pCN/TNs. However, excessive redshift of absorption wavelength band could reduce the photocatalytic activity. The •O2 -, h+, •OH, and 1O2 could be generated, and the former two played major roles, while the latter two played supporting roles in the degradation of TC. Furthermore, the CQDs could promote the production of more reactive species. TC molecule was broken down into small molecules via ring-opening reactions or/and the cleavage of the main carbon bond. The antibacterial activities of treaded solutions and eco-toxicities were detected and predicted by bacteriostatic test and ECOSAR model. This work offered a new sight for the design of excellent photo-catalysts for organic pollutants degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: Photo-catalysis as an effective technology has been widely used to remove organic pollutants [1,2]. The excellent photo-catalysts should possess high photocatalytic activity, stability, and cheapness, which are important in practical applications. The n-type semiconductors, including TiO2, WO3, and Fe2O3, are regarded as prototype photo-catalysts for the degradation of various pollutants, because of their high chemical stability and conduction band edge with a potential level [3–5]. However, these materials only absorb ultraviolet light in the full solar spectra (ultraviolet light accounts for 5%) and the efficiency of rapid recombination of photo-generated electron-holes (e--h+) is excited by ultraviolet light, which limits their practical applications.

TiO2 was accidentally discovered to split water under irradiation in 1972 [6,7]. Being the earliest milestone in photo-catalysis, TiO2 is still being intensively studied, which can provide sufficient potential to drive the redox reaction due to its high band-gap [8]. In addition, the stability and biological nontoxicity make the low price TiO2 material commercially applicable. To better exploit solar energy, some researchers were dedicated to enhancing the absorption of visible light by TiO2, such as changing its structures [1,9], doping with metal and/or nonmetal [10,11], and constructing TiO2-based heterojunction [12,13]. Now, construction of organic-inorganic hybrid heterojunction has been aroused wide concern [14]. Our previous study proved that the heterojunction of carbon nitride nanosheets/bismuth tungstate not only promoted the transfer of electrons and holes, but also inhibited their recombination, which effectively improved photocatalytic activity [15].

Graphitic phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) with a narrow band-gap has been considered as a next-generation photo-catalyst [16,17]. Besides abundant reserves and visible-light response, g-C3N4 also possesses excellent photo-stability and chemical tenability, which are superior to other narrow band-gap semiconductor materials [18]. Therefore, construction of well-fabricated g-C3N4/TiO2 with enhanced visible-light response is a development trend [19,20]. At present, various strategies had been successfully used to construct g-C3N4/TiO2, such as co-calcination, hydrothermal treatment, and microwave-assisted synthesis methods [19,21,22]. In addition, nanoparticle/nanosheet (0D/2D) [23], nanotube/nanosheet (1D/2D) [24], and core-shell [25] heterojunctions had been reported. The ingeniously designed heterojunctions could not only increase the absorption of visible light range, but also promote the separation of photo-generated electron-holes and inhibit their recombination. However, the heterojunction of g-C3N4/TiO2 encountered inevitable traffic congestion of charge carriers at interfaces [22,26].

Due to the superior biocompatibility, high stability, low cost, electron acceptor, and transport properties, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new kind of carbon nanomaterials had been introduced to various fields including electronics, catalysis, and others [27,28]. In recent years, CQDs have been used in the construction of photo-catalysts [27,29,30]. The CQDs as a component of photo-catalysts can build nanoscale junctions with close contacts due to their small sizes. The photo-induced charge carriers can transfer to each other between different semiconductors through CQDs, which can effectively solve congestion of charge carriers at interfaces. Previous studies had proved that CQDs could not only promote the absorption of light, but also receive electrons from other semiconductors [1,31].

In this study, a porous three-dimensional/nanosheet heterojunction of g-C3N4/TiO2 was designed with CQDs as the electron transmission body. For providing a large contact area to facilitate the migration of charge carriers at contact, the TiO2 nanosheets (TNs) was used and the simple co-calcination method was explored. The photocatalytic performance of CQD-pCN/TNs was evaluated by degradation of tetracycline (TC) under visible light irradiation, and the possible photocatalytic mechanism of degradation was deeply analyzed. In addition, the produced intermediates were identified and the possible degradation pathways were proposed, and the antibacterial activities of tread solutions and eco-toxicities were detected and predicted by bacteriostatic test and ECOSAR model, respectively.

Urea, oxalic acid (OA), citric acid (CA), and tetrabutyl titanate (TBOT) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen fluoride (HF), hydrochloric acid (HCl), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from Macklin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Humic acid (HA), isopropanol (IPA), ammonium oxalate (AO), p-benzoquinone (BQ) and sodium azide (NaN3) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). TC as a simulated pollutant was purchased from National Institute for Drug Control (Beijing, China). And all other reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The ultrapure water (UP-water, 18.2 MΩ•cm, 25 °C) was used in the whole experiment except otherwise specified.

The prepared methods of TNs and CQDs were the same as previous research [1]. The sample CN was graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), which was labeled as CNs. The CNs were prepared by annealing method with urea, which was heated to 550 °C at 3 °C/min and then kept under an N2 atmosphere for 5 h. For porous carbon nitride/titanium dioxide nanosheets (pCN/TNs), OA (4 g), urea (6 g), and TNs (1 g) were simply mixed by grinding method to obtain powders, and the heating conditions were the same as that of prepared CNs. The porous carbon nitride (pCNs) was obtained without adding TNs at the same conditions. A certain amount of CQDs was added into the suspension containing 400 mg pCN/TNs, and the suspension was stirred for 12 h and dried at 60 °C overnight to obtain CQD-pCN/TNs. And the theoretical amounts of CQDs were 0%, 0.5%, 1%, and 2% in the composite materials. The details on the characterization of the instrument model and test conditions were provided in Supplementary Material, Text S1.

The photocatalytic performances of prepared materials were evaluated by degradation of TC under 500 W Xenon lamp irradiation. The specific operation steps and parameters were the same as in previous research [1]. The analytical methods were shown in Text S2. All data were obtained via the mean values of three parallel experiments.

The trapping experiments were implemented to detect the reactive species and judge their roles in the photocatalytic process. IPA, AO, BQ, or NaN3 (each 3 mmol/L) as hydroxyl radicals (•OH), holes (h+), superoxide radicals (•O2 –) or singlet oxygen (1O2) inhibitors was added into the reaction system, separately. Although BQ as a colored inhibitor had an effect on degradation, its effect was almost negligible under low concentration conditions. In addition, ESR (electron spin-resonance spectroscopy) was used to further verify the generated reactive species.

The electrochemical performances of prepared photo-catalysts were detected by a three-electrode system on the CHI604 electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd.). The operation steps and experimental parameters were the same as those in previous research [1].

The bacteriostatic activities of TC and its intermediates were tested via the zone of inhibition method. The specific operation steps were the same as in previous research [1]. In addition, the eco-toxicities of produced intermediates were calculated via the ECOSAR model.

The size and morphology of prepared samples were detected by HRTEM (high-resolution transmission electron microscopy) and SEM (scanning electron microscope) with EDS mapping (energy dispersive spectrometer). As shown in Fig. S1, the structure of the TiO2 sample was sheet-like, and the lattice spacing was about 0.34 nm. The surface of the sample was loose and porous for pCN/TNs sample (Fig. S2(a)), and there were no particles on its surface (Fig. S2(b)). After pCN/TNs sample was modified by CQDs sample, there was no obvious change in morphology ( Fig. 1). The particles were distributed on the surface of pCN/TNs (Fig. 1(a)). To analyze the size distribution of CQDs samples, the TEM of single CQDs samples was shown in Fig. S3(a). The average diameter of the particles was calculated as approximately 4.58 nm (Fig. S3 (b)) according to the Nano-Measurer 1.2 software [1,32], indicating that the particles might be CQDs. However, the lattice spacing of CQDs had not been found (Fig. 1(b)), which might be caused by their presence in an amorphous state [15]. According to elemental composition analysis (Fig. 1(c-d)), C, N, O, and Ti existed in CQD-pCN/TNs sample.

The crystal phases and crystal structures of prepared TNs, CNs, pCN/TNs, 0.5% CQD-pCN/TNs, 1% CQD-pCN/TNs, and 2% CQD-pCN/TNs samples were detected by XRD (X-ray diffraction). As shown in Fig. 2, a diffraction peak of CNs sample at 27.5° corresponded to g-C3N4 (002) plane for inter-planar stacking of the aromatic system [33]. And the other peak located at 12.8° was assignable to the (100) plane for triazine repeat units [34], which was consistent with FT-IR of a sharp peak at 813 cm- 1 (Fig. S4). Moreover, the diffraction peaks of TNs sample at 25.2°, 37.7°, 48.1°, 53.8°, 55.1°, 62.7°, and 70.2° were responsible for planes of (101), (004), (200), (105) (211) (204), and (220), respectively (PDF No. 21-1272) [1]. For the XRD characterization of pCN/TNs, a distinct peak was located at 10°-23°, which was attributed to g-C3N4, while the typical characteristic peak at 25.2° corresponded to TNs. The intensities of peaks were slightly reduced and the characteristic peak (101) had slightly shifted (Fig. S5), which might be caused by the interaction between pCNs and TNs [35]. In addition, there were no obvious peaks of CQDs in the prepared CQD-pCN/TNs, which could be explained as a small amount of CQDs or an amorphous form existing [1].

The XPS (X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy) was employed to further explain the surface composition and chemical state of prepared CNs, pCN/TNs, and CQD-pCN/TNs samples. Survey XPS spectra displayed that the element C (284.8 eV), N (398.4 eV), O (529.99 eV), and Ti (458.6 eV) existed in prepared CQD-pCN/TNs samples (Fig. S6), which was consistent with SEM-EDS mapping analysis. When pCN/TNs was modified by CQDs, the ratio of C/N increased from 9.14 to 10.62, indicating that excess carbon might come from CQDs. The C 1s spectra of CNs, pCN/TNs, and CQD-pCN/TNs were shown in Fig. 3(a). The characteristic peaks at 284.8 ± 0.1 and 288.7 ± 0.1 eV could be indexed to C-C and sp2-hybridized carbon within triazine rings (N-CN) [36]. For CNs with a porous structure, the peak of N-CN had a noticeable shift, which might be caused by generated C-O, indicating that the porous structure was oxygen-rich. While the peak position (N-CN) did not shift after the introduction of CQDs, indicating that CQDs showed negligible effect on the original structure of triazine rings, which was consistent with the results of XRD. In addition, the position of C-O shifted from 285.95 eV to 285.85 eV after CQDs modification, which could be caused by the interaction of C-OH with C atoms to generate the C-O-C bond between pCNs and CQDs. Fig. 3(b) showed N 1s spectra. For pCN/TNs and CQD-pCN/TNs, there were three peaks corresponding to that of pure CNs, and the C-NC, N-(C3), and CN-H were located at 398 ± 0.1, 399.2 ± 0.1, and 400.9 ± 0.1 eV, respectively [37]. While the positions of peaks had shifted, which might be caused by the porous oxygen-rich structure of CNs and interaction with TNs. Additionally, a new peak of N-O appeared at 399.6 ± 0.1 eV for pCN/TNs, indicating that CNs formed an oxygen-rich structure [38]. Fig. 3(c) presented O 1s spectra. For CNs, two peaks located at 531.7 and 532.9 eV, respectively, which were corresponding to CO (carbon dioxide or incomplete polymerization of organic matter) and O-H (adsorbed water) [39,40]. When OA and urea as precursors were mixed with TNs to roast and generate pCN/TNs sample, new peaks located at 529.8 ± 0.1 (Ti-O) and 532.0 ± 0.1 eV (C-O) came out. After CQDs were modified, the intensity of CO increased, the part of CO might come from the CQDs [41]. As shown in Fig. 3(d), the Ti 2p spectra of pCN/TNs and CQD-pCN/TNs included Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 at 458.5 ± 0.1 and 464.2 ± 0.1 eV, respectively. As known, the positions of characteristic peaks of pure TiO2 were located at 458.5 and 464.2 eV, repectively [42]. Based on the above results, the positions of both peaks were not significantly offset, indicating that the chemical state of Ti was not altered regardless of the presence of pCNs and CQDs.

The photocatalytic performances of prepared CNs, TNs, pCN/TNs, 0.5% CQD-pCN/TNs, 1% CQD-pCN/TNs, and 2% CQD-pCN/TNs were investigated to degradation of TC. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the degradation ratio was negligible without photo-catalyst under visible light irradiation. And the degradation ratios were about 32.2%, 54.0%, and 65.5%, respectively in presence of CNs, TNs, pCN/TNs under 120 min irradiation. Obviously, after pCN/TNs were modified by CQDs, their photocatalytic performances were significantly increased, and the degradation ratios increased from 83.5% to 86.2% as loading amounts of CQDs increased from 0.5% to 1%. However, the degradation ratio decreased to 81.7%, when the amounts of loading CQDs reached to 2%. The pseudo-first-order kinetic was used to investigate the reaction rate (Eq. (S2)). The fitting curves with obtained parameters were shown in Fig. S7. And the degradation kinetic constants of these materials were displayed in Fig. 4(b). The 1% CQD-pCN/TNs possessed the highest degradation rate for TC, which was 5.46, 2.52, 1.92, 1.11, and 1.31 times of CNs, TNs, pCN/TNs, 0.5% CQD-pCN/TNs, and 2% CQD-pCN/TNs, respectively. The CQDs as electron transmission body could enhance the photocatalytic activity of designed heterojunction of g-C3N4/TiO2, and 1% was chosen as the CQDs dosage for design to obtain the best photocatalytic performance.

Importantly, the photocatalytic performance of 1% CQD-pCN/TNs for degradation of TC was compared with other materials, and the results were shown in Table S1. The photo-catalysts including CQD@B-ECN [43], N-CQDs/MCNx [44], CQD/Bi2MoO6 [45], 3D perylene diimide [46], CQD-C3N4 [47], MIL-100(Fe)/Polyaniline [48], and Bi12O17Cl2/Ag/AgFeO2 [49] were used to degradation of TC with the degradation ratios 68% (120 min), 89% (120 min), 65% (120 min), 80% (150 min), 78.6% (240 min), 84% (120 min), and 94.1% (60 min), respectively. When compared with these pioneer works, the CQD-pCN/TNs, obtained by simple preparation process, possessed a relatedly high competitiveness in efficiency (86.2%, 120 min) and might be potentially practically used in environmental purification.

The photocatalytic activity was closely related to light utilization efficiency, electron transfer efficiency, and electron-hole recombination efficiency. To analyze the light utilization efficiency, the UV-vis DRS was implemented and the results were shown in Fig. 5(a). For TNs, the wavelength at which light was absorbed was below 375 nm, which was consistent with previous report [1]. As for pCN/TNs, the absorption wavelength widen to 470 nm, indicating that the constructed heterojunction structure between pCNs and TNs could expand the absorption wavelength. After pCN/TNs was sensitized by CQDs, the absorption edges further enlarged to the infrared region. The redox capacity of hole-electrons was closely related to the band-gap of photo-catalyst, and the redshift of the absorption spectrum could decrease the band-gap. The CQDs as the electron acceptors did not affect the valance band (VB) of semiconductors. When the band-gap decrease would cause the conduction band (CB) to increase, which weaken the reduction ability of electrons on the CB [41]. The disadvantage could be compensated by increasing the absorption of visible light. The redshift of absorption edge within a certain range could enhance photocatalytic activity. However, when the visible light could not compensate for the redshift effect, the photocatalytic activity was reduced [41]. For CQDs (0.5%, 1%, and 2%) modified pCN/TNs, the best photocatalytic activity was obtained at the 1%. As for the modification below 1% CQDs, the redshift effect could be compensated by increasing the absorption of visible light, while above 1%, the redshift effect could not be compensated, which would lead to a decrease in the catalytic activity. Through Tauc's plot method, the band gaps of prepared TNs, pCNs, and pCN/TNs were about 3.48, 3.32, and 2.75 eV, respectively (Fig. 5(b)). Furthermore, according to empirical equations (Text S3), the E
CB and E
VB for pCNs were -1.44 and 1.88 eV, respectively, while -0.43 and 3.05 eV, respectively, for TNs.

The PL spectra could reveal the separation and recombination rate of the photo-induced electron-hole, therefore, the fluorescence test was performed under 368 nm light excitation. The recombination rate of the photo-induced electron-hole pairs was positively correlated with the intensity of the fluorescent signal [50]. As shown in Fig. 5(c), CNs possessed the strongest fluorescence intensity at 400-700 nm, indicating that photo-generated electron-holes recombination was faster. While the structure of CNs became porous and oxygen-rich, the fluorescence intensity obviously decreased. As for pCN/TNs, the fluorescence intensity further decreased. The photo-generated electron-holes could transfer between semiconductors due to the different energy bands, which could effectively inhibit the photo-generated electron-holes recombination. When CQDs were dropped on the pCN/TNs, the intensity of the peak decreased apparently. The CQDs could accept electrons from pCN/TNs to suppress the recombination of electron-holes. The fluorescence intensity was lowest for 1% CQD-pCN/TNs compared to those for other materials, indicating the electron-holes recombination rate for the 1% CQD-pCN/TNs was the slowest. Inhibition of the recombination of electron-hole could enhance the photo-catalytic activity. Compared with other photo-catalysts, the constructed 1% CQD-pCN/TNs could well inhibit the recombination of electron-holes, and possessed the best photocatalytic performance for degrading TC. Meanwhile, the experimental results also proved the above conclusion.

The EIS Nyquist described interfacial electron transfer of photo-catalyst, and the radius of the arc was related to the charge transfer process at the electrode/electrolyte interface [51]. The larger the radius of arc was, the slower the electron transfer was, indicating that the electron-holes could not be effectively separated [52,53]. As shown in Fig. 5(d), at the same conditions, the radius of arcs for prepared TNs, pCNs, pCN/TNs, 2% CQD-pCN/TNs, 0.5% CQD-pCN/TNs, and 1% CQD-pCN/TNs gradually decreased, which suggested that 1% CQD-pCN/TNs possessed excellent abilities of separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs and faster interfacial electron transfer. The rapid transfer of electron-holes could effectively inhibit their recombination, which further enhanced the redox capacity of the system to degrade pollutants. Therefore, the photocatalytic performance of 1% CQD-pCN/TNs was superior to those of other systems.

Previous studies had proved that the reactive species including •OH, •O2 -, h+, and 1O2 would be generated [15,53]. To investigate the roles of reactive species for degradation of TC, the reactive species trapping experiment was hired. In this study, the IPA, BQ, OA, and NaN3 were used as the inhibitors of •OH, •O2 -, h+, and 1O2, respectively [54,55]. The photocatalytic performance of CQD-pCN/TNs was shown in Fig. 6(a) in the system. With the presence of each IPA, BQ, OA or NaN3 in the concentration of 3 mmol/L, the degradation ratios decreased to 68%, 15.7%, 48.4%, and 81%, respectively, and their reaction kinetic constants were reduced to 0.00761, 0.00094, 0.00467, and 0.0121 min- 1, respectively, lower than 86.2% and 0.0141 min- 1 without the inhibitors (Figs. 6(b) and S8). Thus the contribution of •O2 -, h+, •OH, and 1O2 to the degradation would be 81.79%, 43.85%, 21.11%, and 6.05%, respectively, and the total inhibition rate was 152.8%. The reason for the total inhibition rate exceeding 100% was due to the fact that there were overlaps in the effects of the inhibitors on reactive species [56]. The results approved the generation of •O2 -, h+, •OH, and 1O2 in the system, and suggested the main role of •O2 - and h+, and the supporting role of •OH. The 1O2 made little difference in degrading TC, which was consistent with the observation by Zhang et al. [46], who found the addition of the NaN3 into the photocatalytic system resulted in only a negligible inhibition rate of 0.099%. ESR spectra were used to further verify the roles of reactive species in the photocatalytic process. Fig. 6(c) and (d) showed the spectra of •O2 - and •OH with and without light irradiation. There was no obvious signal for the •O2 - in the absence of light. The system could generate a clear signal of •O2 - under visible light irradiation, and the signal intensity would increase as the irradiation time increased. The system could not produce •OH without visible light irradiation, and there was a weak signal with typical characteristic peaks of 1:2:2:1 with irradiation. The signal intensity of •OH was weaker than that of •O2 -. Based on the above results, the photocatalytic system would produce the •O2 - and •OH under light irradiation, but the amount of •O2 - was more than that of •OH, which was consistent with the reactive species trapping experiment analysis.

To confirm that the CQDs could promote the generation of reactive species in the CQD-pCN/TNs, the ESR spectra of CQD-pCN/TNs and pCN/TNs were compared. As shown in Fig. S9, in the dark, there were no singles for the •O2 - and •OH in the systems with CQD-pCN/TNs and pCN/TNs. When turn on the Xe light, the obvious singles for the •O2 - and •OH came out. At the same condition, the signal intensity of •O2 - in the system with CQD-pCN/TNs was stronger than that with pCN/TNs, and the •OH also had the same phenomenon. The above results indicated that CQDs modified pCN/TNs could promote the production of more reactive species. In addition, the signal intensity of •O2 - was stronger than that of •OH in the system with pCN/TNs, which was consistent with that with CQD-pCN/TNs, indicating that •O2 - was major reactive species for the degradation of TC.

The possible photocatalytic mechanism of degradation of TC by CQD-pCN/TNs was proposed in Fig. 7. Based on the analysis of UV-vis DRS, the band gaps of prepared TNs and pCNs were about 3.48 and 3.32 eV, respectively. And the CB and VB potentials for TN were about 3.05 and -0.43 eV, respectively, while for pCNs, they were about -1.44 and 1.88 eV, respectively. The CB potential of TN was higher than that of CNs, which made electrons to transfer from pCNs to TNs. The VB potential of pCNs was lower than that of TNs, which made holes to transfer from TNs to pCNs. The transmission of photo-induced electron-holes could effectively inhibit their recombination, which contributed to the improvement of photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the CQDs accepted electrons generated from pCNs, and then the electrons transferred to TNs, which could effectively solve the congestion of charge carriers at interfaces between pCNs and TNs. And CQDs could convert long-wavelength light into short-wavelength light for absorption by sensitized material to improve light utilization. The CQD-pCN/TNs were excited by visible light irradiation to photo-induced electrons and holes. The generated electrons could react with O2 to produce •O2 -, and the holes could react with H2O/-OH to produce •OH. However, the E
VB of pCNs and TNs were about 1.88 and 3.05 eV, respectively, the system just generated the •OH via the reduction by holes on the TNs due to the E
•OH/H2O (2.68 eV) and E
•OH/-OH (1.99 eV) [25,56]. This conclusion also explained •OH played a supporting role in the degradation of TC.

The water qualities are important parameters for practical application, therefore, the different sources of water as background waters are hired to investigate the effect on the photocatalytic performance of CQD-pCN/TNs for degradation of TC. As shown in Fig. 8(a), the degradation ratios were 86.2%, 50.2%, 60.0%, and 80.0% in UP-water, secondary effluent, river water, and tap-water, respectively, and their corresponding degradation kinetic constants were 0.01414, 0.00433, 0.00557, or 0.01144 min- 1, respectively (Figs. 8(b) and S10). The most obvious inhibition effect on degradation was secondary effluent, followed by river water, which might be caused by the existing competitive organic matters and/or anion inhibitors.

Based on the effect of different background water on the degradation of TC, the inorganic anions including NaCl, Na2SO4, and Na2CO3, and organic matter (humic acid, HA) are employed to investigate the effects on photocatalytic performance. As shown in Fig. 8(c), the degradation ratios decreased with adding the inorganic anions, and the order of influence degradation was Na2CO3 > NaCl> Na2SO4, which was consistent with the previous study [57]. The CO3 2- or/and HCO3 - could consume generated reactive species [58], which caused the degradation ratios to decrease from 86.2% to 50.4%. As for NaCl and Na2SO4, the Cl- and/or SO4 2- could compete with TC for adsorption sites to decrease the degradation ratio [59]. In addition, the competitive adsorption between TC and Na+ on the adsorption sites might also lead to lower photocatalytic performance. HA as a typical dissolved organic matter with rich functional groups affected the photocatalytic performance. As shown in Fig. 8(d), the degradation ratios decreased with increasing the concentration of HA. And the degradation ratio could be reduced by 30.2% in presence of 25 mg/L HA. This phenomenon could be attributed to the following reasons: (1) the light-shielding effect of HA (because of the darker color and insoluble particles of HA, the light could not reach the surface of photo-catalyst effectively and could not cause the photocatalytic reaction) [60]; (2) the scavenging effect of HA on reactive species (HA could compete with TC and react with reactive species, which lead to reducing the degradation efficiency) [61]. Similarly, Liu et al. [60] also approved that the HA could weaken the photocatalytic performance for degradation of hydroperfluorocarboxylic acids.

Fig. S11 showed the degradation ratio of TC and TOC removal efficiency during the photocatalytic process. The TOC decreased gradually with increasing irradiation time. The TOC removal efficiency was about 25.7%, while the TC degradation ratio could reach 86.2% within 120 min irradiation. The results showed that the TC molecular was broken down into small substances, and a just small part of TC was mineralized to form CO2 and H2O. The 3D-EEM (three-dimension excitation emission matrix) was used to analyze the changes of substances during the degradation process. As shown in Fig. 9(a), there was no extra signal presented in the initial solution of TC. After 10 min irradiation, Two obvious fluorescence peaks came out at Ex/Em (excitation spectrum/emission spectrum) = 300-340/305-330 nm and Ex/Em = 330-365/350-355 nm (Fig. 9(b)), which were corresponded to the aromatic hydrocarbons and humic acid-like substances [62]. The results illustrated that the structure of TC was broken to generate the aromatic hydrocarbons and humic acid-like substances. With increasing irradiation time, the intensity of the peaks increased (Fig. 9(c-f)), indicating that the TC molecules continued to be decomposed, and/or some generated intermediates were difficult to be degraded and easy to be accumulated. In order to better study and explore the intermediates, the HPLC-MS/MS was employed.

As shown in Fig. S12, there was a main peak with m/z = 445 before visible light irradiation, which corresponded to the TC molecule, indicating that the structure of TC was not broken down without light irradiation. As irradiation time increased, the intensity of this peak gradually decreased and new peaks appeared, indicating that the intermediates were generated. The information on possible intermediates including molecular structures and molecular weights were shown in Table S2, and the possible pathways were proposed ( Fig. 10).

The photocatalytic system could generate strong oxidizing reactive species including •O2 -, h+, •OH, and 1O2, which mainly attacked double bonds, aromatic rings, and amino group. Therefore, the intermediates were generated via ring-opening reactions or/and the cleavage of the main carbon bond [63]. Pathway I mainly included two processes of the detachments of N-demethylation and ring-opening reactions. Firstly, because of the lower N-C bond energy in the TC molecule (m/z = 445), the holes attacked the N-C bond to generate Compound III (m/z = 417) via loss of one N-methyl. And then the higher energy groups of Compound III were further attacked by the holes to form Compound IV (m/z = 353). At last, the Compound IV was decomposed into small molecular of Compound V (m/z = 255) through h+, •O2 – and •OH joint attack [64]. Secondly, the generated Compound III was attacked by the 1O2 and the double-bond of the aromatic ring broke, producing Compound VI (m/z = 481). Finally, according to the cleavage of double-bond and attack of reactive species, Compound VII (m/z = 234) and VIII (m/z = 216) were produced [63]. Pathway II: intermediates of Compound I (m/z = 344) and Compound II (m/z = 279) formation processes mainly included deamination, hydroxylation, and ring-opening. The intermediates of Compound III, IV, and VI, and Compound V, VII, and VIII belonged to humic acid-like substances and aromatic hydrocarbons, respectively. They would generate fluorescence signals at Ex/Em = 300-340/305-330 nm and Ex/Em = 330-365/350-355 nm, respectively, which were agreed with the signals in 3D EEMs (Fig. 9). It was noticeable that these compounds could not be further degraded easily, thus the toxicity of the treated solution should be considered.

The generated intermediates might be more poisonous than the parent compound, and the antibacterial activity and eco-toxicity of the treated solutions were evaluated. As shown in Figs. S13 and S14. The diameters of zone of inhibition were negatively correlated with the antibacterial activities of treated solutions. The diameters increased with irradiation time before 10 min, and the value was about 14.5 mm at 10 min. And then the diameters decreased with increasing the irradiation times, the value was about 6.75 mm at 120 min. The above results indicated that the toxicities of treated solutions firstly increased and then decreased. Although the antibacterial activity of the treated solution at 120 min was closed to that of the blank solution, it still possessed toxicity, and the toxicity was still not negligible.

The ECOSAR model was employed to further analyze the eco-toxicities of TC and intermediates, and it could predict the eco-toxicities for fish, daphnia, and green algae of half-maximal effective concentration (LC50 or EC50) and chronic toxicity value (ChV) [65]. The predicted values of TC and its intermediates were shown in Table S3 in the neutral condition. According to the Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labeling of Chemicals, the levels were established (very toxic (LC50/EC50/ChV≤1 mg/L); toxic (1 mg/L< LC50/EC50/ChV≤10 mg/L); harmful (10 mg/L<LC50/EC50/ChV≤100 mg/L); and not harmful (LC50/EC50/ChV<100 mg/L)). The TC and its intermediates showed different acute toxicity levels, but they were all at a no-harmful level to organisms. The chronic toxicity of the intermediates except for Compound IV and VIII were not harmful. And Compound VIII only had chronic toxicity to Daphnia (82.833 mg/L). Although TC and its intermediates were unlikely to have an acute toxicity effect on the aquatic organisms, chronic toxicity for the aquatic organisms still needed to be taken seriously. It is therefore important to extend the reaction phase for total mineralization to eliminate this hazard, by taking full account of the potential for toxic intermediates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability and reusability of CQD-pCN/TNs
ce:section.ce:para: The stability and reusability of material was important parameters for practical application. Hence, the repeated cycle tests were used. The used CQD-pCN/TNs was washed three times by UP-water, and dried in the oven at 60 °C for later use. After five cycles (Fig. S15), the photocatalytic performance for degradation of TC was decreased from 86.2% to 78.3%, which may be due to incomplete desorption of TC or/and intermediate. Although, the photocatalytic performance decreased slightly, its activity was still higher than those of some photo-catalysts (CQD@B-ECN [43] and CQD/Bi2MoO6 [45]), indicating that the CQD-pCN/TNs possessed excellent reusability. The XRD of used CQD-pCN/TNs were shown in Fig. S16. The crystal phase and crystal structure of used CQD-pCN/TNs did not change significantly. The above results indicated that the CQD-pCN/TNs had good stability. Importantly, the leached Ti in the treated solution was detected by the ICP-MS after photo-degradation of TC at a concentration of 0.0625 ng/L.

The CQD sensitized pCN/TNs had been successfully prepared via the simple co-calcination method, and 1% was chosen as the CQDs dosage for design to obtain the best photocatalytic performance. TC removal ratio could reach 86.2%, while 25.7% of TOC degradation ratio was achieved. The CQDs could enlarge the absorption range of light and improve the utilization rate of light. The pCN/TNs could not only quickly promote the separation of electron-holes, but also effectively inhibit the recombination of electron-holes. The •O2 - and h+ had great contributions via attacking functional groups of double bonds, aromatic rings, and the amino group of TC, while the 1O2 and •OH had support roles in the degradation of TC. The antibacterial activities of treated solutions were reduced compared to TC initial solution after photocatalytic degradation and the intermediates had no eco-toxicities except Compound VIII (chronic toxicity to Daphnia, 82.833 mg/L). In addition, the NaCl, Na2SO4, Na2CO3, and HA in the solution could inhibit the degradation of TC. Importantly, the prepared CQD-pCN/TNs possessed excellent stability and reusability.

Xinghao Liu: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Ying Yang: Writing – review & editing. Zhaoguang Yang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration. Haipu Li: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration.
